text.title: Test Doubles: Mock, Stub, Spy, Fake, Dummy
text.summary: Five test double types (Meszaros vocabulary): Dummy fills a parameter, Stub returns canned values, Spy records calls, Fake is a working simplified implementation, Mock verifies expected interactions. Stubs test state; Mocks test behavior.

rule.statement: Select the simplest double that satisfies the test: Dummy if the parameter is never used, Stub if you need a controlled return value, Mock if you need to assert the dependency was called correctly, Fake if a real but simplified implementation is acceptable, Spy when you need post-hoc verification without strict pre-specification.
rule.rationale: Over-mocking creates brittle tests that break on implementation change, not behavior change. A Fake (e.g., in-memory repository) is more robust than a Mock with many pre-specified expectations.

list.item: Dummy — fills unused parameters; no behavior needed
Stub — returns predefined value; test checks returned state
Spy — records calls for after-the-fact assertion; flexible
Fake — working implementation (e.g., in-memory DB); realistic behavior
Mock — pre-specified expectations; fails if call sequence or args don't match

rule.statement: Use a Stub when the test assertion is on the return value of the code under test; use a Mock when the test assertion is that a specific method was called on the dependency.
rule.rationale: Mixing the two purposes makes tests test implementation details when they should test behavior, leading to high maintenance cost.

example.code:
# STUB — test cares about return value, not whether repo was called def test_user_service_returns_none_for_unknown_email(user_service, repo_stub): repo_stub.find_by_email.return_value = None
result = user_service.find_by_email("unknown@example.com")
assert result is None # asserting state
# MOCK — test cares that email was sent def test_user_service_sends_welcome_email_on_registration(user_service, email_mock): user_service.register(email="new@example.com", name="New")
email_mock.send_welcome.assert_called_once_with("new@example.com") # asserting behavior

antipattern.description: Asserting on mock call counts for behavior that is an implementation detail, not a business requirement.
antipattern.reason: The test becomes coupled to the exact implementation, breaking on refactoring even when behavior is preserved.

rule.statement: Prefer a Fake (in-memory repository, in-process message bus) over a Mock for integration boundaries that are called many times or in complex sequences.
rule.rationale: A Fake with correct semantics is more faithful than a Mock with many pre-programmed return values. It tests the same logic without requiring a real external service.
